coredata.dc:title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon using graphene Quantum dots-chitosan as the precursor and its supercapacitive behaviors
coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Wensheng
Fu, Renjun
Ji, Hong
Kong, Yong
Xu, Yueguo
Qin, Yong
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon (N-C) is pyrolytically prepared by using the nanocomposites of graphene Quantum dots (GQDs) and chitosan (CS) as the precursor. Due to the existence of GQDs nanofiller, the three-dimensional (3D) interconnected frameworks of CS are well preserved after the pyrolysis treatment; meanwhile, CS in the nanocomposites functions as nitrogen source for the N-C. The obtained N-C exhibits a considerable specific capacitance (545Fg−1 at 1Ag−1), high rate capability and excellent cyclic stability (88.9% capacitance retention after 5000cycles at 10Ag−1) when it is used as the electrode materials in supercapacitors. The well-preserved 3D frameworks and N-doping are believed to be responsible for the excellent supercapacitive behaviors of the N-C.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon
Graphene Quantum dots
Chitosan
3D frameworks
Supercapacitor electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing the preparation of N-C using GQDs-CS as the precursor.
TGA and DTG of the GQDs-CS precursor in nitrogen atmosphere at the rate of 20°Cmin−1.
XRD patterns of N-C and the GQDs-CS precursor.
FT-IR spectra of N-C and GQDs-CS.
(A) Full XPS spectra of N-C and GQDs-CS. High resolution C 1s (B) and N 1s (C) XPS spectra of N-C.
FESEM images of GQDs-CS (A), N-C (B) and carbon from the pyrolysis of CS alone (C).
TEM images of GQDs-CS (A) and N-C (B).
Cyclic voltammograms of N-C at varying scan rates in 1M H2SO4.
Galvanostatic charge/discharge curves of N-C in 1M H2SO4 at different current densities of 1, 2, 3, 5, 10 and 20Ag−1.
Cyclic stability testing of N-C for 5000cycles in 1M H2SO4.

head.ce:title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon using graphene Quantum dots-chitosan as the precursor and its supercapacitive behaviors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center, School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Changzhou Key Laboratory of Large Plastic Parts Intelligence Manufacturing, Changzhou College of Information Technology, Changzhou 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Changzhou Key Laboratory of Large Plastic Parts Intelligence Manufacturing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Changshu Changel Chemical Co., LTD., Changshu 215522, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon (N-C) is pyrolytically prepared by using the nanocomposites of graphene Quantum dots (GQDs) and chitosan (CS) as the precursor. Due to the existence of GQDs nanofiller, the three-dimensional (3D) interconnected frameworks of CS are well preserved after the pyrolysis treatment; meanwhile, CS in the nanocomposites functions as nitrogen source for the N-C. The obtained N-C exhibits a considerable specific capacitance (545Fg−1 at 1Ag−1), high rate capability and excellent cyclic stability (88.9% capacitance retention after 5000cycles at 10Ag−1) when it is used as the electrode materials in supercapacitors. The well-preserved 3D frameworks and N-doping are believed to be responsible for the excellent supercapacitive behaviors of the N-C.

ce:section.ce:para: Porous materials with three-dimensional (3D) interconnected ordered structure have been extensively utilized in various industrial processes [1]. Among the porous materials, porous carbon is one of the promising candidates, which is technically important for a variety of applications such as double-layer capacitors, catalyst supports, adsorption and separation systems, hydrogen storage materials and electrochemical sensors [2–4]. In general, porous carbon is prepared via carbonization of various raw materials, and the commonly used raw materials in commercial practice are biomass materials such as coconut shell [5], hazelnut shell [6] and eucalyptus kraft lignin [7].

Chitosan (CS) is one of the most plentiful nature polysaccharides on earth, and it has attracted increasing attention in the fields of biomedical and tissue engineering owing to its excellent biocompatibility, non-toxicity and biodegradability [8–10]. More recently, nitrogen-containing CS-based carbon as an electrode material for high-performance supercapacitors have been reported [11,12]. However, the extensive applications of porous carbon from calcined CS as the electrode materials in supercapacitors still remain a challenging task, which is probably due to the lack of 3D interconnected porous framework in the resultant porous carbon.

Graphene Quantum dots (GQDs) are a class of “zero-dimensional” carbon materials, and they exhibit similar properties to graphene since GQDs belong to graphene sheets smaller than 100nm [13]. That is to say, GQDs also possess extraordinary mechanical flexibility and chemical stability, and the two outstanding properties of GQDs make it a promising candidate used as nanofillers in the preparation of nanocomposites. Recently, the nanocomposites of CS and GQDs were successfully synthesized by our group [14–16], in which the GQDs function as the nanofillers and can be well-dispersed within the CS matrix.

Inspired by the well-dispersed status of GQDs nanofillers in the CS matrix, porous carbon is pyrolytically prepared by using the nanocomposites of GQDs and CS (GQDs-CS) as the precursor in this work, and the as-prepared porous carbon exhibits a 3D interconnected framework. In addition, CS in the precursor can function as the nitrogen source for doping. The obtained nitrogen-doped carbon (N-C) is further applied as the supercapacitor electrodes and exhibits excellent supercapacitive behaviors including specific capacitance, rate capability and cyclic stability, which can be ascribed to the well-preserved 3D interconnected frameworks in the resultant N-C as well as nitrogen-doping.

Chitosan (CS, 85% deacetylation degree), citric acid monohydrate and other reagents not mentioned were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (SCRC, China). All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water of 18.2MΩ (Milli-Q, Millipore).

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential thermogravimetric analysis (DTG) were carried on a TG 209 F3 thermal analyzer (Netzsch, Germany) in nitrogen atmosphere (20°Cmin−1). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns and the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of N-C and GQDs-CS were recorded on a D/max 2500PC diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) and a FTIR-8400S spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The morphologies of N-C and GQDs-CS were characterized with a Supra55 field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Zeiss, Germany) and a JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan), respectively. All the electrochemical measurements including cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) curves were performed on a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation.

According to the bottom-up method previously reported [17], GODs were prepared by using citric acid as the precursor. 2g of citric acid monohydrate was put into a crucible and heated to 200°C in a muffle furnace. After 30min, the products (GODs) were cooled down to room temperature and then dissolved in 10mL of water under sonification. Next, 20mg of CS was dissolved in 10mL of 0.1M acetic acid, and the obtained CS solution (2mgmL−1) was mixed with the yellow GQDs solution under stirring. The mixture was continuously stirred for 10min, and then 1M NaOH solution was added into the mixture for the deposition of the GQDs-CS nanocomposites. Finally, the precipitated GQDs-CS was freeze-dried in a vacuum lyophilizer at −50°C for 24h and then calcined at 750°C for 6h in nitrogen atmosphere (at the rate of 20°Cmin−1) for the preparation of N-C. The schematic illustration showing the preparation of the N-C using GQDs-CS as the precursor is shown in Fig. 1 .

All electrochemical experiments were carried out in a conventional three-electrode cell consisting of N-C loaded glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as the working electrode, a Pt foil and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The electrolyte was 1M H2SO4 aqueous solution. The working electrode was prepared by the following procedures: 5mg of the as-synthesized N-C was dispersed in 5mL of water, and then 7μL of the dispersion was dropped onto the surface of a GCE (3mm in diameter) and left to be air dried (active materials loading: ~100μgcm−2). Both the cyclic voltammograms and the GCD curves of the N-C modified GCE (N-C/GCE) were collected over the potential range from 0 to 0.4V. The cyclic stability test was carried out by repeating the GCD measurements at the current density of 10Ag−1. The specific capacitance (C) of the N-C based supercapacitor can be evaluated from the GCD curves according to the following equation:
(1)
C =
I × t
m × V
where I represents the current density (Ag−1), t is the time of discharge (s), m is the mass of N-C loaded on the GCE (g), and V is the potential window during the discharge process (V) [18–20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: TGA and DTG of GQDs-CS precursor
ce:section.ce:para: TGA and DTG of the GQDs-CS precursor in nitrogen atmosphere are investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 , two obvious DTG peaks appear at 70°C and 300°C, respectively. The former is attributed to the evaporation of adsorbed water, and the latter could be ascribed to the thermal decomposition of the GQDs-CS. The total weight loss resulted from the decomposition can reach 90% at 750°C, and then it remains almost unchanged with further increasing temperature. Therefore, the calcination of the GQDs-CS precursor is carried out at 750°C.

The XRD patterns of the as-prepared N-C as well as the GQDs-CS precursor are shown in Fig. 3 . For GQDs-CS, the diffraction peaks at 2θ =11.7° and 18.6° are attributed to the Form I and Form II crystals of CS [21], and another weak and broad diffraction peak around 24.2° might be ascribed to the thinness and disordered stacking of GQDs [22–24]. The diffraction peaks of CS disappear completely after calcination, meanwhile, the peak at around 24.2° is strengthened significantly. It is also noted that a new broad diffraction peak appear at around 43.6° on the XRD pattern of N-C. Since the diffraction peaks at around 24.2° and 43.6° could be assigned to the (002) and (100) crystalline planes of graphitic carbon [25,26], it is concluded that the GQDs-CS precursor is successfully converted to graphitic carbon after calcination.

Fig. 4 shows the FT-IR spectra of N-C and GQDs-CS. For GQDs-CS, the peak at 1718cm−1 is attributed to the symmetric stretching vibrations of CO from the COOH groups on GQDs [27], and the peak at 1075cm−1 might be ascribed to the COH stretching vibrations of CS. However, the intensities of the two peaks are greatly decreased after calcination, suggesting CS in the GQDs-CS precursor is pyrolyzed to graphitic carbon and most COOH groups are removed from GQDs. More importantly, new peaks ascribed to the CN stretching (1612cm−1) and CN stretching (1174cm−1) [28] are clearly observed, indicating that N atoms are successfully transferred from CS to the graphitic carbon lattice during the calcination process.

The chemical composition of the as-prepared N-C and the GQDs-CS nanocomposites is studied by XPS. O, N and C elements are detected at 532.1, 400.2 and 285.1eV, respectively, for both N-C and GQDs-CS (Fig. 5A). However, the relative atomic percent content of each atom is quite different for the two samples. The contents of O, N and C elements are 35.37%, 6.64% and 57.99% in GQDs-CS, and 7.30%, 3.48% and 89.22% in N-C. This result clearly indicates that during the calcination process, most oxygen-containing groups are removed from GQDs accompanied with the increase in the content of C element, which agrees well with the FT-IR results. The resolved C 1s and N 1s XPS spectra of N-C are shown in Fig. 5B and C, respectively. Three components of C 1s can be found in the N-C at 284.8, 286.0 and 287.2eV (Fig. 5B), which correspond to the CC bonds of sp3-C, the carbon atoms bonded to hydroxyl groups (COH) and carbonyl groups (CO), respectively [29]. High resolution N 1s spectra show three peaks located at 398.5, 400.8, and 401.9eV (Fig. 5C), corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively [30]. As previously reported, the presence of N atoms in carbon network would introduce pseudocapacitance effects to the electrode substrates [31], and therefore it is expected that the as-prepared N-C might possess superior supercapacitive behaviors through combining electrochemical double layer capacitance and pseudocapacitance.

Fig. 6 shows the FESEM images of GQDs-CS nanocomposites (Fig. 6A) and N-C (Fig. 6B). For the GQDs-CS nanocomposites, a 3D interconnected ordered structure is observed. The appearance of such 3D interconnected frameworks in the precursor could be ascribed to the interfacial interactions induced by electrostatic attractions and H-bondings between CS and GQDs, which has been discussed in detail in our previous research [15]. Interestingly, a quite similar 3D interconnected ordered structure is also observed in the N-C. That is to say, the 3D interconnected frameworks of GQDs-CS are well preserved in the N-C after the pyrolysis treatment, which is most likely due to the existence of GQDs nanofillers in the precursor. To verify our assumption, the FESEM image of the carbon from the pyrolysis of CS alone is also recorded for a comparison (Fig. 6C). As can be seen, the 3D interconnected porous frameworks disappear completely in the carbon prepared through the pyrolysis of CS alone. This comparison clearly indicates that the GQDs nanofillers in the precursor play a crucial role in the preservation of the 3D interconnected porous frameworks during the pyrolysis treatment.

The TEM images of GQDs-CS and N-C are shown in Fig. 7 . For the GQDs-CS nanocomposites, the GQDs nanofillers (black dots) are well-dispersed within the CS matrix (Fig. 7A). However, high temperature (750°C in this work) can induce graphitization of the precursor totally, since the black dots of GQDs disappear after pyrolysis and there are no differences in the resultant N-C (Fig. 7B). This result demonstrates that the homogeneous nanoarchitecture of carbon materials can be obtained via graphitization of GQDs-CS at sufficiently high temperatures.

The cyclic voltammograms of N-C in 1M H2SO4 at varying scan rates (from 10 to 100mVs−1 with an interval of 10mVs−1) are shown in Fig. 8 . All the cyclic voltammograms exhibit a roughly rectangular shape especially at low scan rates, suggesting that the capacitance of N-C is predominantly determined by the electrochemical double layer [32]; meanwhile, the roughly rectangular shape might be ascribed to the reversible redox of N functionalities generated from a moderate fraction of pseudocapacitance [33]. It is also found that as the scan rate is increased gradually, the cyclic voltammograms almost maintain the same shape, indicating that the N-C possesses good rate capability [34].

The capacitive performances of the N-C are further studied, and the GCD curves of the N-C in 1M H2SO4 at different current densities (from 1 to 20Ag−1) are recorded. As shown in Fig. 9 , these GCD curves display almost symmetrical linear shape with humps in the potential range from 0 to 0.4V, implying that the specific capacitance of the as-prepared N-C is combined with electrochemical double layer contribution originated from the hierarchical porous carbon and pseudocapacitance contribution originated from the doped N element. According to Eq. (1) described in Section 2.3, the specific capacitances of N-C are calculated to be 545 and 175Fg−1 at the current densities of 1 and 20Ag−1, respectively. Obviously, the high specific capacitance of N-C at low current density (1Ag−1) is attributed to the synergistic effects of well-preserved 3D interconnected frameworks of N-C and nitrogen-doping.

Finally, the cyclic stability of the N-C is studied in 1M H2SO4 at the current density of 10Ag−1. As shown in Fig. 10 , the N-C displays a retention of as high as 88.9% of its initial capacitance after 5000cycles. Such high cyclic stability of the N-C might be ascribed to the presence of GQD nanofillers which function as a rigid support and maintain the 3D interconnected frameworks of N-C during the continuous and cycling charge/discharge.

In summary, 3D interconnected frameworks of GQDs-CS can be well preserved after the pyrolysis treatment due to the presence of GQDs nanofillers, and the obtained N-C is then used as the electrode materials for supercapacitors. On one hand, the 3D interconnected nanostructure of N-C offers an easy access for electrolyte ions and result in good capacitive behaviors; on the other hand, CS in the precursor functions as the nitrogen source for the doping of N-C, and therefore the specific capacitance of N-C is combined with electrochemical double layer contribution and pseudocapacitance contribution. The as-prepared N-C also exhibits excellent cyclic stability, which could be attributed to the presence of GQD nanofillers that function as a rigid support and maintain the 3D interconnected frameworks of N-C during the cycling.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the financial supports from Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20171194, BK20150269), Future Research Project of Jiangsu Province (BE2017088), Changzhou high-tech Key Laboratory (CM20153001) and Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).
